summary: Gruber lays out his predictions ahead of Apple's WWDC 2009 keynote, covering the iPhone 3GS, a $99 iPhone, tethering, Snow Leopard pricing, and the rumored Apple tablet. He correctly anticipates the iPhone 3GS name and specs, and predicts the $99 iPhone will simply be a discounted iPhone 3G rather than a new device. The bulk of his analysis focuses on Snow Leopard's marketing dilemma: how to sell an OS update focused on under-the-hood improvements rather than flashy new features. He concludes that the rumored 'Marble' UI redesign won't ship with Snow Leopard, and that the Apple tablet is real but not ready to announce.
key_points: The iPhone 3GS will bring faster processors, doubled RAM and storage, a video camera, and better battery life in essentially the same form factor
The $99 iPhone will be the existing 3G at a reduced price, not a new smaller device — Apple will eventually make an iPhone Nano, but not yet
Snow Leopard's central challenge is marketing: how to price and sell an OS update built around optimization rather than new features
The 'Marble' UI redesign is real but won't ship with Snow Leopard because developers need time to adapt, pushing it to 10.7
The Apple tablet is real but not ready to announce — patience is required
money_quotes: I have no idea what the S stands for.

I would wager heavily on (b) — that the new iPhone 3GS models will not replace the 3G, but rather assume the flagship position while the year-old 3G slides down to the second spot in the product lineup.
It'd be nice if it just worked, with no additional charge over the current data plan. But "it'd be nice" seldom happens with phone carriers.
I look forward to never paying for hotel or airport Wi-Fi service again.
OS X is here for the long haul — it is the foundation of Apple's entire business for the foreseeable future.
Maybe it's not true that people only pay for features upgrades.
But Apple is not Microsoft. OS upgrade revenue is a nice extra for Apple, not a core part of its business.
If it looks new it is new, from a normal person's perspective.
key_insight: Snow Leopard's focus on optimization over features created an unprecedented marketing puzzle for Apple, revealing that OS upgrade pricing is a nice extra for Apple rather than a core business the way it is for Microsoft.
